cot_prefix: Okay, let's try to figure out this question. The story is about an employer who's settling accounts with his children's governess, Julia. He starts by saying they agreed on 30 rubles a month, but Julia says it was 40. He insists on 30 and goes on calculating how much she's owed, subtracting various amounts for Sundays, holidays, sick days, her toothache, broken items, and other incidents. Eventually, he ends up giving her 11 rubles, but then gets angry because she thanks him, thinking she's been cheated. He then gives her 80 rubles instead, because he's upset she didn't protest the initial amount. The question is asking why the employer made all those criticisms about Julia's work. The options are A to D. So, why would he criticize her? Let's break it down. The employer is trying to justify paying her less. He lists all the reasons to deduct money. But then, when Julia doesn't protest and just thanks him, he gets angry, saying he wanted to teach her a lesson. At the end, he gives her the full 80 rubles, which was probably the full amount she was owed (since he had initially given her 11 after all the deductions, but then realized he was being unfair and corrected it). The key part is when he says, "I played a little joke on you, a cruel lesson, just to teach you..." So he was trying to make her realize she was being cheated and protest. His intention was to test her by underpaying and seeing if she would stand up for herself. When she didn't, he felt bad and corrected it. So the answer is probably C. Option B says he wanted to pay as little as possible.
